title: Chiococca nocturna
intro: Chiococca nocturna, also known as Night-blooming Gratiola or Night-blooming Chiococca, is a plant species native to the tropical forests of Central and South America, characterized by its small white flowers that bloom at night. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat a range of health issues, including fever, cough, and respiratory problems, due to its anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties. The bioactive constituents of Chiococca nocturna include flavonoids, terpenoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made from Chiococca nocturna include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat various respiratory and inflammatory conditions.
taxonomy: Chiococca nocturna is classified within the Apocynaceae family, which is a group of flowering plants commonly known as dogbane or oleander. This family is further divided into several subfamilies, including Apocynoideae, Asclepiadoideae, and Periplocoideae. Chiococca nocturna is specifically placed within the subfamily Apocynoideae. The genus Chiococca belongs to the tribe Apocyninae, a group of plants that are mostly found in tropical and subtropical regions. The classification of Chiococca nocturna is an important aspect of understanding its evolutionary history and relationships with other plant species.
